Greek letters fill the height of the rim in a single line
Neat, regular deeply incised letters with pronounced terminations to the strokes and a broad module. Alpha has straight bar; epsilon has slightly shorter middle bar.
second or early first century BCE
1[Ἀπ]ελλᾶ (vac.2)ἱερ[ατεύσασα]
Three letters filling the sloping face of the rim
Thinly incised, regular and broad letters, with simple terminations to the strokes. Alpha with straight bar, pi with equal verticals, slighly over-hanging horizontal.
1[---]ΑΠΠ̣[---]
Traces of three lines of Greek letters
Worn, thinly incised lunate letters. Alpha with crossbar descending to left; lunate epsilon; lunate omega; kappa with short bars.

Remains of four lines of Greek letters, filling what is left of the face of the stone.
Neat reguular v-cut lettes with simple serifs. Alpha has extension at the top; epsilon is lunate; kappa has full length arms; lunate sigma; uncial omega; phi has rhomboid eye.

1[--- Ν]εικάνδρου
2[--- ἔζησεν] ἔτη με´
3[--- ὑιὸ]ς πατρί
Single line of text, centred, on the front face, 11 cm up from the lower edge.
Letters hard to discern in photograph: Mu is large and wide of equal length sloping strokes; zeta formed with vertical upright stroke; omega small and widely open.
1Μέλισα Ζωπύρου

Remains of three lines of large Greek letters with regular left margin. The text is not perpendicular to the face of the block as it survives, either implying poor layout or else the block has been recut.

Large wide and square letters, regularly set out, without notable serifs. Alpha has broken bar; epsilon has shorter middle bar; kappa has short arms; omicron is slightly smaller than other letters and above the line; pi has equal length vertical strokes and slight overlap of the horizontal; rho has a small closed loop
later third or second century BCE

1[Ε]ὐπραξ̣[ία] Ἡ̣ρ̣α̣κλε[ί-]
2δαι Ἡρα[κ]λείδα
3ἐποίη̣[σε] (vac.)
Text fairly crudely and lightly incised across the upper part of the flat face below the cornice, beginning at the left margin of the stone.
1Εὐμάρηι ΑΡΝ[---]
Funerary inscription for the son of Artemon
Remains of two lines, damaged at either end, seemingly centered

Regular square letters of even height. Alpha has broken bar; epsilon has shorter middle bar; mu has vertical first and last, with the middle strokes meeting mid-way above the baseline; omicron is full size; rho has small eye;  omega is broad and lunate.
More likely late hellenistic than early imperial (lunate omega not before the second century BCE and rare on stone so early)
Antiquarium e museo civico di Aci Castello

Two lines of large Greek letters across the face above the moulding, of which the beginnings and ends are lost.

Plain square letters of regular size without serifs. Mu has vertical first and last, midstrokes descending almost to the line; Alpha appears to have straight bar. Pi has equal length hastae, with overlapping top bar.

Stone stamp bearing a Latin name
A limestone stmpaing device with a Latin personal name from Morgantiina
D. Alex Walthall and Kalle Korhonen, ‘A Limestone Stmpaing Device with a Latin Personal Name from Morgantiina’, Zeitschrift Für Papyrologie Und Epigrafik 232 (2024): 309–11
Text is retrograde filling the long face of the stamp
Neatly incised letters of slightly varying height and use of ligatures with short vertical interpuncts. Slight curvature to the strokes and tails rather than serifs.
Archaeological context ranges between c.275 BCE and 50 CE, lettering Late Republican

Remains of two columns of text (the left margin of column 2 is preserved and regular), text engraved using the punched dots technique.
Epsilon has shorter middle bar; kappa has short arms; omicron is very small and midline; pi has shorter second vertical; sigma is slightly open four-bar.

1Οἱ ἀλειφόμενο[ι ---]
2Ὄρθωνα Φορμίων[---]
Statue base in honour of Olympis Mestos

The inscription is set out over four lines on the front face of the block on its upper half. The text is roughly centred, lines 1-3 filling the face, line 4 a single word approximately centered. Spacing between words is apparent and increases significantly in line 3.

The letters are regular and with terminal points rather than serfis, and are inscribed in parts in what is almost a punch style, rather than a chiselled groove. Alpha has broken bar; epsilon has mostly equal legnth cross-bars; theta has a full-width cross bar; kappa has full length bars; mu has vertical first and last, three-quarter length mid-strokes; omicron is full-size; pi has eual length verticals; rho has fair-sized closed eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom strokes; omega is rhomboid and closed.
Later Hellenistic, later second or first century BCE?

The text is engraved on the upper surface of the stone (a second text on the front face); a large circular hole intrudes into the first visible line of the text, and the stone is broken away on the right side of the upper part, so the original extent of the text is unclear, but there appears to be a vacat after the third visible line

Plain letters of the middle Hellenistic period: Pi has verticals of equal length; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; omicron is small and mid-line; epsilon has a short middle stroke; mu has vertical first and last strokes, while the middle point only descends part way; alpha has both broken and straight bar
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo della cultura e delle Arti Figurative Bizantine e Normanne

Text is engraved in the upper left corner of the front face of the stele; the text is incomplete on the right sides as the right upper corner is lost; however there is clear vacat below.

Letters of the mid-Hellenistic period: epsilon has short middle bar; omicron is small and mid-line; mu has vertical first and last strokes, the mid-point descends half-way; alpha is formed with both broken and straight bar; sigma has approximately parallel top and bottom strokes; omega is open; theta formed with a central point.

Traces of four lines, showing signs of compression to the right margin on line 3

From the drawing, appear deeply cut letters without marked serifs, of a slightly irregular module. Alpha with straight bar; M with vertical outer strokes and all full length; omicron nearly full size; omega closed and rhomboid. Line 1 appears to be clearly bigger than lines 2-4. (measurements taken from the drawing)

3[---] τοῦ Τίμωνος
4[---]νο[.]

(Following Orsi's apograph in his taccuino) Small regular neat letters with terminations to the strokes but no signficant serifs. Alpha has straight bar; Epsilon has slightly shortened middle bar; Zeta is vertical with wide top and bottom horizontal strokes; Kappa has slightly shortened arms; Mu has vertical first and last, with the middle strokes almost reaching to the base line; omicron is full-size; Sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; Omega is round full-sized, and almost closed.
2nd or 1st century BCE by the lettering which is later Hellenistic
Orsi, 2018, I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4

Fragment of a list of names
Remains of four lines of Greek letters, with a vacat above; guidelines to top and bottom of each line, irregular right margin.

Standard later Hellenistic letters, of moderately square module, with simple terminal serifs. Epsilon with shorter middle bar; Mu with four full length, oblique strokes; omicron slightly smaller and above the line (12-13 mm); sigma with parallel top and bottom strokes.

Traces of six lines of Greek, with evidence of intralinear spacing (line 3) and interlinear spacing (blank 'line' after line 4). Guidelines are visible to lines 3 and 7.

Small, deeply cut letters; the degree of surface wear makes it difficult to assess the use of serifs. Alpha probably broken bar; epsilon with bars of equal length; rhomboid omicron and phi; four-bar sigma with horizontal outer strokes.
